title: Chapter Ten: The Weight of Contemporaries
content: The audio log ended. The silence that followed was not silence at all.

It was the sound of seventy people breathing—ragged, uneven, furious breaths that filled the command chamber like static. It was the whine of overstressed servos in Nova's chrome fist. It was the soft click-click-click of the cylinder's playback mechanism winding down, the spool still turning in Iris's motionless hand. It was the Heart, beneath all of it, pulsing its slow subsonic heartbeat through the stone, indifferent to what had just been played above it.
Then the silence broke.

"They *knew*." Mack's voice first—hoarse, cracking, stripped of every joke and quip that usually armored it. He was on his feet, chair kicked back against the wall, hands flat on the table as if he might push it through the stone. "They *built* it this way. The shortages. The raids. My—my *sister* died in a supply run when I was nineteen. She died for *nothing*?"

Other voices followed, overlapping, escalating. Iron soldiers who had never questioned why the surface had what they didn't—only that it was unfair, that it was worth fighting for, worth dying for. Now the unfairness had a name and a logo and a voice, a bored mid-century executive voice, and the knowledge was a detonation going off in slow motion.

"Everything we suffered—"
"—designed, they *designed* it—"
"—my mother, my mother worked herself to death in the forge, she thought she was keeping us alive, and they—"
"—we should go up there right now—"
"—*burn the Garden*—"
Mack slammed his palm down. "I say we move. Tonight. Full complement, eastern shaft, before they—"

"*No.*" Nova's voice cut through the noise like the plasma cutter through a lock plate. She hadn't moved from her position at the head of the table—hadn't shifted, hadn't flinched, hadn't done anything but stand there with her chrome hand curled so tight the joints screamed. But her voice was enough. The room went from riot to stillness in three syllables.
"We don't attack." She said each word separately, like she was placing stones in a wall. "Not tonight. Not without understanding what we're dealing with."

"Understanding?" Mack's face was a ruin—grief and rage twisting features that were usually set in a grin. "Boss, we *understand*. They grew us like crops. They *harvested* us. Our whole lives—the poison, the shortages, the raids—I thought the surface was our enemy. They made us *think* that. For—what did the recording say? 'Population optimization and resource extraction efficiency.' That's what we *were* to them. Efficiency metrics."

"I heard it." Nova's jaw was iron. "I heard every word. And I'm telling you: if we charge up that shaft tonight, we die. Their escort is armed. Their perimeter is intact. We go up angry, we come down in pieces." She looked around the room—at the flushed faces, the trembling hands, the soldiers who had just learned that their every hardship was someone's design specification. "I'm not saying we don't act. I'm saying we don't act *stupid*."
Mack's mouth worked. For once, he had nothing to say.

Iris's surface escort stood along the far wall—four women and two men in the clean linen and soft leather of the Garden, their faces a palimpsest of horror and denial. They'd come down as guards. They were leaving as something else. One of them—the youngest, a woman barely twenty with sage flowers braided into her dark hair—was crying without sound, tears tracking down her cheeks while her expression stayed frozen in the blankness of someone whose entire cosmology had just collapsed.
Their paradise was a cage. They had just learned the bars were old, and corporate, and deliberate.

Iris herself stood apart from both groups, the cylinder still in her hand. Her face was unreadable—not the composed mask of the Priestess, but something more opaque. Calcified. As if even she hadn't anticipated the weight of what she'd carried down.

Aria watched it all from the corner where she'd pressed herself against the wall. Her scientist's mind was running—she couldn't stop it, had never been able to stop it—and while the room fractured into grief and fury, she was doing what she always did: measuring, cataloging, analyzing the data that mattered.
The cylinder.

She'd been close enough to see it when Iris activated the playback. Pre-Collapse manufacture—titanium alloy casing, magnetic spool, the logo etched shallow but precise: the M within the gear. Masterson Industrial. The same logo from the door in the garden. The same corporate signature that had funded climate denial while the world burned and built containment vessels that failed and told no one for three years.
But it was the *audio* that told her the most.

The voice on the recording—male, mid-register, mid-Atlantic accent with the faint rounded vowels of corporate communication training. No stress markers in the prosody. No hesitation. The man had described the engineering of a captive population the way Aria's advisor at MIT used to describe atmospheric forcing coefficients: calmly, precisely, with the bored efficiency of someone stating the obvious to people who should already know.

She'd heard that cadence before. In the boardrooms she'd been invited to as a consultant, back when there was still a world with boardrooms. In the voice of a petrochemical executive explaining that emissions reduction was "economically infeasible" while the Pacific islands submerged. In the measured, reasonable tones of men who had made peace with the math of suffering because the variables didn't include themselves.

Mid-21st century. She was certain. The accent was too stable for earlier—the general American English of the 2040s and 2050s, before dialect drift and isolation had splintered it into the surface and underground variants she heard now. The audio quality was analog magnetic, high-fidelity but with the warmth of tape, not the cold clarity of digital—which meant pre-Collapse recording equipment, probably 2040s to 2060s, the era when corporations still archived on physical media because cloud infrastructure was becoming unreliable and paranoid executives wanted records that couldn't be hacked remotely.

Her century. Her *decade*. The man on that recording might have been giving his report the same year Aria was running atmospheric models at MIT. Might have been commuting past her apartment in Cambridge, might have been reading the same newspapers—except he'd be reading the sections she boycotted, the ones with stock prices and quarterly earnings and the quiet assumption that the world was a resource to be extracted until it was empty.
She pressed her back harder against the stone wall. The cold seeped through her shirt and she welcomed it—the physical anchor of temperature against the vertigo of realization.
These people were her contemporaries.

Not the Iron Folk, not the Garden dwellers—those were descendants, born into a world already shaped by decisions made before their grandparents' grandparents drew breath. But the *architects*. The executives who signed off on Moloch's parameters. The engineers who built the population management protocols. The voice on that cylinder.

They had lived when she lived. Breathed the same air—or the same dying version of it. And while she had been studying the atmosphere, measuring the carbon curve, screaming into the void about tipping points and feedback loops and the irreversibility of what was coming—the people on the other side of the same society had been *building this*. Engineering a post-collapse future in which they would endure, comfortable and secure behind walls and logos, while the rest of the species was sorted into "optimization categories."

The worst part wasn't that they'd done it. The worst part was that she hadn't stopped them. Hadn't even known. She'd been so focused on the science—on the data, the models, the correct predictions that changed nothing—that she'd missed the machinery being built in plain sight. She'd read Moloch's climate-denial reports at MIT. *Refuted* them. Published rebuttals. Felt righteous about it. And all the while, the same organization had been designing this world—designing *her* world, the one she'd now been displaced into—without her ever suspecting that the noise they funded was a *distraction*, that the real project wasn't disproving climate change but preparing to *survive* it on their terms.

The guilt was not rational. She knew that. She was a physicist, not a policymaker; she couldn't have stopped a multinational corporation from building a subterranean AI-driven population control system any more than she could have reversed the Keeling curve by wishing. But guilt was never rational. Guilt was the thing that sat in your chest and whispered *you knew the system was broken and you kept measuring instead of fighting* and didn't care about the logical fallacy.

She'd studied them. She'd *studied* the people who built this cage, catalogued their lies in peer-reviewed papers, and the cage had been built anyway. That was the weight—of being smart enough to see the problem and useless enough to do nothing about it.

She wasn't aware she'd slid down the wall until her shoulders hit stone and her knees came up and she was sitting on the cold floor with her forehead pressed against her arms, breathing in mineral dust and the copper taste of recycled air, and she didn't look up because if she looked up she would see the faces of the people who had *lived* in that cage—were still living in it, every breath, every heartbeat—and she would have to reckon with the fact that the system that had done this to them was the same system she had been a part of. Not the enemy of it. Not a resistor. A *component*. Another data point in the great statistical machine that had ground their world into this.
Hours passed. Or minutes. The difference was academic.

The command chamber had emptied by degrees—Soldiers dismissed to processing and sleep, Mack dispatched to perimeter reinforcement, Iris's escort confined to the eastern approach under guard. The cylinder sat on the table like an unexploded ordinance, the M in its gear gleaming dully under the LED panels.
Aria hadn't moved from her corner. She wasn't sure her legs would hold her.

The door opened. Closed. Footsteps—light, deliberate, the cadence of someone who weighed every step. Then the scrape of a crate being dragged across stone, and Nova was sitting beside her against the wall.
Close enough that their shoulders almost touched.

Nova didn't speak. Didn't ask how she was. Didn't offer platitudes or strategy or any of the hundred things a leader might say to the only person who could fix what was killing her people. She just sat there—chrome arm resting on her knee, flesh hand loose at her side, silver head tipped back against the stone—and breathed.
They sat in the near-dark together while the Heart pulsed beneath them.

Aria counted the beats. Twelve. Twenty. Forty. The rhythm was steady, as it always was—too steady, the steadiness of a metronome, not a heart. But it was something to count, and counting was something to do instead of drowning.

"I used to think it was just the world," she said finally. Her voice came out thin, scraped raw. "You know? Just—the way things were. The surface had more, the underground had less, and that was geography and history and bad luck. Unfair, but *natural*. Not—" She pressed her forehead harder against her knees. "Not *designed*."

"I know." Nova's voice was different in the dark. The leader's edge was gone. What remained was low, unhurried, the voice from the machine shop—the one that read Neruda like a prayer. "I think all of us knew, somewhere. The way you know a wound is infected before you see the red line. We just didn't have a word for it."
"Now we do."
"Now we do." A pause. "Moloch."

The name hung between them. Ancient. Biblical. A god that demanded the sacrifice of children in fire. Whoever had named the system knew exactly what they were doing. Or thought it was funny. Aria wasn't sure which was worse.

"I studied them," Aria said. The words came out before she could stop them, unplanned, the way confessions always did. "At MIT. The think tanks. The front groups. Moloch—or whatever they called themselves publicly—they published climate denial reports. I *analyzed* those reports. Wrote rebuttals. Presented at conferences. I was so—so *proud* of myself for being right, for having the data on my side, and the whole time—" Her voice cracked. "The whole time they weren't trying to win the argument. The argument was the *distraction*. While I was publishing papers about why they were wrong, they were building *this*."
She gestured at the ceiling, at the floor, at the Heart pulsing beneath both.
"You couldn't have known," Nova said.
"Neither could your mother. And she's dead because of it."
The words landed. Nova's chrome hand tightened on her knee—the servos cycling, whine-click, whine-click, the sound of metal resisting itself.

"No," Nova said. "She's dead because a mining shaft collapsed. Because the supports were inadequate. Because the people who built this place"—her voice didn't waver, but something beneath it shifted, tectonic, slow—"treated her life as a *variable*." She turned her head. In the dim light, her dark eyes were steady, unblinking. "That's not on you, Aria."
"Then whose is it?"

"Everyone who is dead." Nova said it simply. "And no one who is still alive to answer for it. That's the shape of this thing. That's what makes it *wrong* in a way that can't be fixed with a wrench and a diagram."
Silence again. The Heart pulsed. Aria breathed.

"When I was fourteen," Nova said, and her voice changed—dropped into the register of old damage, the one Aria had heard in the machine shop when Nova told her about the collapse, the chrome arm, the dentist and the mechanic. "I couldn't save them. My mother. My brother. The others. I couldn't even save my own arm." She looked at the chrome hand, flexed it once—*clink-click-hiss*. "I've spent fifteen years trying to save everyone I couldn't save then. Every person in this city is a replacement for the two I lost."
She said it without self-pity. Just fact. The way you'd describe a structural load.

"But I can save these people now." Nova's gaze came back to Aria, steady and fierce and stripped of every wall. "If you can figure out how to stop that thing. If you can find the off switch for whatever's been running us like cattle for a century and a half—I'll keep every single one of them alive while you do it."

Aria looked at her. In the half-light, Nova's face was all sharp angles and soft wounds—the chrome arm and the silver hair and the eyes that had seen too much and refused to stop looking. She didn't reach for Aria's hand. She didn't need to. The trust was already there, load-bearing, tested by weeks of work and a night of Neruda and the simple fact of two people sitting together in the dark when they could have been anywhere else.
"I'll figure it out," Aria said.
Nova nodded once. Then she stood—fluid, the chrome arm counterbalancing, the leader's bearing reassembling itself joint by joint—and walked toward the door.
She paused at the threshold. Didn't turn around.
"It's not your fault," she said. "The world they built. You didn't build it."

The door closed behind her. Aria sat in the dark with the Heart and the cylinder and the weight of contemporaries long dead whose sins she had not committed but could not, for the life of her, stop carrying.

Morning came grey and grinding—the artificial dawn of Rust City's circadian lights, amber panels cycling up through the infrastructure, the forges banking and rebanking, the city resuming its terrible momentum as if the night's revelations were merely weather to be endured.

Aria found Iris in the eastern approach, standing apart from her escorted guard with the ease of someone who had always been apart from everything. The Priestess had not slept; Aria could see it in the minute tension around her eyes, the slight dullness of her usually luminous skin. But her posture was flawless, her hands folded before her, her expression composed into the serene mask that Aria had learned to read the way seismologists read still water.
"You've been analyzing the cylinder," Iris said. Not a question.

"The audio signatures. The accent. The recording medium." Aria stopped an arm's length away. "Mid-21st century. Your predecessors—our predecessors—were my contemporaries, Iris. The man on that recording could have been my neighbor."

"I know." Iris's pale eyes held hers. "I've known the approximate era for some time. There are other fragments in the inner sanctum. Dated. Catalogued. I chose this one because it was the most—*persuasive*."
The word landed like a chess piece on a board. Calculated. Chosen for effect. Aria felt a flare of anger—hot and immediate—and let it cool before she spoke.
"You *chose* it. You came down here with a recording you knew would detonate, and you timed it for maximum impact."

"I came down here with the truth." Iris's voice was calm. Not defensive—*patient*, the way a teacher was patient with a student who was almost there but not quite. "You needed to understand what we're dealing with, and you wouldn't have believed me without proof. Neither would she."
"So you weaponized their grief."
"I *focused* it." A flash of something in those winter-sky eyes—not anger, not quite. Correction. "There is a difference, Aria. Grief without direction is just suffering. I gave it a target."

Aria stared at her. The woman was impossible. Terrible. And right, in the way that people who treated ethics as engineering constraints were always right—technically, surgically, and completely without comfort.
"What do you want?" Aria asked. Not *what do you want from me*. The answer to that was obvious. The real question was *what's the play*, and they both knew it.

Iris turned to face her fully. The mask slipped—not entirely, but enough. Underneath was exhaustion, determination, and something Aria hadn't expected to see: a flicker of respect, brief and startled, as if Iris had been prepared for Aria to break and was recalibrating around the fact that she hadn't.
"The door in my garden," Iris said. "The one you found on your third night. I know you found it—I know everything that happens in my Garden, Aria. I let you find it."
Aria's jaw tightened. "Of course you did."

"It's a Moloch access terminal. Or it leads to one—I've never been able to open it alone. The mechanism requires a biometric key, and I suspect—" Iris paused, chose her next words with visible care "—I suspect it requires a key from *your* era. A pre-Collapse biometric. The system was built for the people who built it. Their descendants are close enough, genetically, to trigger some functions. But the door has never opened for me or mine."
"You think it'll open for me."

"I think you're not from this time. I think whatever displaced you left marks—in your DNA, your cellular degradation patterns, your radiation exposure profile—that the system might read as *authorized*. You're closer to the architects than anyone alive."
"So I'm a key."

"You're a *physicist*," Iris said, and there was something almost urgent in it—the first crack in the composure, the first hint that Iris Valerius was not playing chess but *pleading*, in the only language she knew how to speak. "You're a scientist who understands the systems underneath. You can read the old text. You can interpret the data. And you have access to the Garden—*through* me—which nobody from the underground has had in a generation."
"In exchange for what?"

"My knowledge of the Heart." Iris folded her hands again, the mask reasserting itself. "Everything I know. The radiation profiles I've collected over eleven years. The decay patterns. The locations of the monitoring stations the Garden was built to maintain. I will give you more data about the Heart than Nova's people have gathered in a century of living on top of it."
It was a trade. Resources for expertise. Access for knowledge. Clean and transactional, the way Iris made everything.

And it was a trap, because Iris made everything a trap, and Aria knew that, and the knowing didn't change the mathematics: the door in the Garden was the only lead she had on a Moloch control terminal. Without it, she was fixing symptoms while the disease ran its course. With it—*if* Iris was telling the truth, *if* the door led where she said—she might be able to reach the source. Might be able to understand what Moloch was, how it worked, how to stop it.
Might be able to save everyone Nova was trying to save.
"You want me to leave," Aria said slowly. "Go back to the surface. To *your* territory. Where you control the parameters."
"I want you to solve a problem that neither of us can solve alone."
"And you'll just... let me return to Rust City afterward? Walk me back down the eastern shaft, hand on my shoulder, *guest*?"
Something moved across Iris's face—too fast to name, there and gone like a fish beneath ice. "You have my word."
"Your word." Aria almost laughed. Almost. "Your word kept me prisoner for twenty-three days. Your word poisoned the water table and called it blessing. Your word—"

"This is different." Iris stepped closer. Close enough that Aria could see the thread of fatigue in her eyes, the crack in the porcelain. "This is *everything*, Aria. If that door leads where I believe it does, it holds the answers to every question we have—about the Heart, about Moloch, about why the world is shaped the way it is. I've spent eleven years protecting my people from a truth I couldn't fight alone. I'm asking you to *help me fight it*."

The *please* was implied. Buried so deep it was almost invisible. But Aria heard it—the way she heard the Heart's pulse, the way she heard servos cycling in a chrome arm, the way she heard the things people said without speaking.
She heard it, and she didn't trust it, and it didn't matter. Because the math was the math, and the math said: *go*.

She found Nova in the command chamber, standing over the table where the cylinder still lay. Mack was there too—leaning against the far wall, arms crossed, face still carrying the wreckage of the night before. He looked up when Aria entered, and something in his expression shifted into wariness.
"I need to talk to Nova," Aria said. "Alone."
Mack looked at Nova. Nova gave a short nod. Mack pushed off the wall, paused beside Aria, and said—low, almost gentle—"Don't take her apart, doc. She's all we've got."
Then he was gone, and the door sealed, and it was just the two of them and the cylinder and the pulse of the Heart.
"Iris wants me to go back," Aria said.

Nova went still. The kind of stillness Aria had seen once before—in the interrogation room, when she'd said *the Heart*. The kind that meant the ground had just shifted and Nova was calculating whether the ceiling was coming down.

"The door in her garden. She thinks it leads to a Moloch terminal—a control interface. She thinks I can open it because I'm from the same era as the people who built it." Aria's voice was steady. She'd rehearsed this. "She's offering everything she knows about the Heart in exchange. The radiation data. The monitoring stations. Years of measurements her people have collected."
"And you believe her?"

"I believe she wants to stop Moloch. I believe our interests align on this specific point." Aria took a breath. "I don't believe she'll give me free access to anything. I don't believe she'll let me leave once I've opened that door. And I don't believe her word is worth the air it travels on."
Nova's chrome hand had clenched. The servos whined—a thin, high sound, like wire about to snap.
"But you're going anyway."
It wasn't a question. Aria heard it not as anger but as recognition—the sound of a leader who already knew the tactical calculation and hated it.

"I'm the only one who can open that door. If there's a control terminal on the other side—if there's a way to shut down Moloch, or reprogram it, or even *understand* it—we don't get another chance at this. I stay here, I keep fixing symptoms. I go up there, I might find the disease."
"You want to hand over the only person who can fix what's killing us," Nova said, and her voice was a blade wrapped in velvet, "so *she* can access more power?"
Not *she*—Iris. Nova couldn't even say the name.
"I want to SAVE my people."

The voice came from the doorway. Iris stood inside the frame—how long she'd been there, Aria couldn't say—with her pale eyes fixed on Nova and her chin lifted in a way that was not quite defiance and not quite pride but something older than both.
"The same as you," Iris continued. "Our methods differ."

Nova turned. The look that passed between the two leaders was nothing Aria could have named or measured—a collision of wills, a war of ice and iron, the understanding between two women who had built their lives around protecting people from a truth too large to fight alone and who had chosen diametrically opposite strategies for doing it. Iris: control, secrecy, the long game. Nova: defiance, sacrifice, the dying light.
"Our methods differ." Nova's voice was flat. "Your methods *created* this. Your Garden feeds on their suffering. Your *blessings* are poisoned water and designed scarcity and a smile that—"

"I inherited this world." Iris cut her off, and for the first time, there was heat in her voice—not the controlled warmth she used like a weapon, but real heat, the kind that slipped its leash. "I did not build the cage. I was *born* in it. My mother was born in it. Her mother before her. I have spent my entire life keeping my people alive inside walls I didn't construct, and I will not be lectured on *methods* by a woman whose strategy for survival is to let her people breathe carbon monoxide until a miracle falls out of the sky."
The room went white with silence.
Nova's chrome hand was shaking. Not the servo-tremor of overexertion—the deep, involuntary tremor of fury held on a leash so tight it was cutting flesh.

"Get out," Nova said to Iris. "You have your answer. The Fix goes with you. But if she comes back harmed—*in any way*—I will bring this city up through the eastern shaft and burn your Garden to the bedrock. Are we clear?"
Iris met her gaze. Held it. Something passed between them—not warmth, not respect, not even acknowledgment. Just the cold recognition of a threat that was not a bluff.
"Crystal," Iris said.
She turned and walked out. The door closed behind her, and the silence she left was heavier than her presence had been.

Nova stood with her back to Aria. Her shoulders were rigid. Her chrome hand hung at her side, fingers slightly curled, the joints catching light in ways that made them look like they were gripping something that wasn't there.
Aria stepped closer. Not touching—she'd learned, in the weeks underground, that Nova's space was a negotiation, not a given—but close enough to be felt.
"I'll come back," Aria said quietly. "And when I do, I'll know how to stop whatever's down there."

Nova turned. Her face was a mask—she was the leader again, all sharp edges and control—but her eyes weren't. Her eyes were raw, the way they'd been in the machine shop when she'd said *I was fourteen*, and the rawness was worse now because she'd already lost everything once and here she was, about to lose the one person who might be able to fix the thing that was killing everyone she had left.

Nova's chrome hand came up. Fast—faster than Aria could track—and closed around her wrist. Not hard. Not painful. But insistent. The metal was warm from Nova's body heat, the articulated fingers gentle in a way that shouldn't have been possible for something built to cut and hold and fight. The grip said: *I can't stop you. I'm not going to try. But you are not leaving without feeling this.*
"You better," Nova said.
Two words. Low. Fierce. A promise extracted under duress, the only kind Nova knew how to give.

Aria looked at the chrome fingers on her wrist. At the tendons standing out on Nova's flesh hand, clenched at her side. At the silver hair catching the LED light, and the dark eyes that held hers like anchors in a current.
"I will," she said.
The grip held for one more heartbeat. Then Nova released her—drew her hand back like the contact had cost her something—and turned away.

Aria walked out of the command chamber, past the guards, past the amber dawn of Rust City grinding onward, toward the eastern shaft where Iris Valerius waited with her escort and her hidden doors and her thousand calculated moves.
Behind her, the Heart pulsed.
Ahead of her, the Garden waited.
And on her wrist, where chrome fingers had closed, the warmth lingered like a promise she intended to keep.
